message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant (B.1.617.2) has been responsible for the current increase in COVID-19 infectivity rate worldwide. We compared the impact of the Delta variant and non-Delta variant on the COVID-19 outcomes in patients from Yogyakarta and Central Java provinces, Indonesia.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We ascertained 161 patients, 69 with the Delta variant and 92 with the non-Delta variant. The Illumina MiSeq next-generation sequencer was used to perform the whole genome sequences of SARS-CoV-2.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The mean age of patients with Delta and the non-Delta variant was 27.3 ± 20.0 and 43.0 ± 20.9 (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =3×10
                    <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                    ). The patients with Delta variant consisted of 23 males and 46 females, while the patients with the non-Delta variant involved 56 males and 36 females (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.001). The Ct value of the Delta variant (18.4 ± 2.9) was significantly lower than the non-Delta variant (19.5
                    <jats:bold>±</jats:bold>
                    3.8) (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.043). There was no significant difference in the hospitalization and mortality of patients with Delta and non-Delta variants (
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.80 and 0.29, respectively). None of the prognostic factors was associated with the hospitalization, except diabetes with an OR of 3.6 (95% CI=1.02-12.5;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.036). Moreover, the patients with the following factors have been associated with higher mortality rate than patients without the factors: age ≥65 years, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease with the OR of 11 (95% CI=3.4-36;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =8×10
                    <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                    ), 27 (95% CI=6.1-118;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =1×10
                    <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                    ), 15.6 (95% CI=5.3-46;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =6×10
                    <jats:sup>−7</jats:sup>
                    ), 12 (95% CI=4-35.3;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =1.2×10
                    <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                    ), and 6.8 (95% CI=2.1-22.1;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.003), respectively. Multivariate analysis showed that age ≥65 years, obesity, diabetes, and hypertension were the strong prognostic factors for the mortality of COVID-19 patients with the OR of 3.6 (95% CI=0.58-21.9;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.028), 16.6 (95% CI=2.5-107.1;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.003), 5.5 (95% CI=1.3-23.7;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.021), and 5.8 (95% CI=1.02-32.8;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.047), respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We show that the patients infected by the SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant have a lower Ct value than the patients infected by the non-Delta variant, implying that the Delta variant has a higher viral load, which might cause a more transmissible virus among humans. However, the Delta variant does not affect the COVID-19 outcomes in our patients. Our study also confirms the older age and comorbidity increase the mortality rate of COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Is the infection of the SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant associated with the outcomes of COVID-19 patients?
